coredata.dc:title: Structure and Delithiation/Lithiation of the Lithium-Rich Layered Oxide Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 as Cathode Material
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2017 The Authors. Published by ESG. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by ESG. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jin-Xia
Gu, Rong-Min
Zhang, Yun-Peng
Yu, Bo-Jun
Wang, Cheng-Yang
Li, Ming-Wei
coredata.dc:description: The typical lithium-rich layered oxide Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 is synthesized via a solid state reaction, and is used as the cathode material for lithium-ion batteries. In situ X-ray diffraction analyses reveal the formation process of Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 crystallites with C2/m monoclinic symmetry. The Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 cathode delivers an initial discharge capacity of 221 mAh g−1 and keeps a reversible capacity of 174 mAh g−1 after 50 cycles. A large capacity loss and the unique charge curve during the first cycle both indicate that Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 was changed into Ni0.2Mn0.8O2 due to irreversible oxygen loss and “rich lithium” extraction. The cathode’s high capacity results from the reversible conversions between Ni0.2Mn0.8O2 and LiNi0.2Mn0.8O2. Both Ni0.2Mn0.8O2 and LiNi0.2Mn0.8O2 keep a stable framework during cycling due to two suggested reasons: (1) some transition metal atoms migrate into the lithium layers and play as the supports between the transition metal layers; (2) 1/4 Mn keeps a stable tetravalent state during cycling and then weakens the Jahn-Teller effect of Mn3+. The predicted reversible capacity of Li[Li(1−2x)/3Ni x Mn(2−x)/3]O2 (0 < x < 0.5) increases from 229 to 280 mAh g−1 with increasing x value, and fairly coincides with the experimental results in this work and literature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-rich layered oxide
cathode
lithium-ion battery

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2017 The Authors. Published by ESG. Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: Structure and Delithiation/Lithiation of the Lithium-Rich Layered Oxide Li[Li0.23 Ni Ni0.15 Mn Mn0.62 ]O ]O2 as Cathode MaterialJin-Xia Wang, 1 3 Rong-Min Gu, 1 3 Yun-Peng Zhang, 1 3 Bo-Jun Yu, 2 3 Cheng-Yang Wang, 2 3 Ming-Wei Li, 1 3 mingweili@tju.edu.cn 1 Department of Chemistry, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China Department of Chemistry Tianjin University Tianjin 300072 China Department of Chemistry, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China 2 Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology Tianjin University Tianjin 300072 China Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China 3 Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin) Tianjin University Tianjin 300072 China Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China The typical lithium-rich layered oxide Li[Li0.23 Ni Ni0.15 Mn Mn0.62 ]O ]O2 is synthesized via a solid state reaction, and is used as the cathode material for lithium-ion batteries. is synthesized via a solid state reaction, and is used as the cathode material for lithium-ion batteries. In situ X-ray diffraction analyses reveal the formation process of Li[Li X-ray diffraction analyses reveal the formation process of Li[Li0.23 Ni Ni0.15 Mn Mn0.62 ]O ]O2 crystallites with crystallites with C 2/ 2/m monoclinic symmetry. The Li[Li monoclinic symmetry. The Li[Li0.23 Ni Ni0.15 Mn Mn0.62 ]O ]O2 cathode delivers an initial discharge capacity of 221 mAh g cathode delivers an initial discharge capacity of 221 mAh gâˆ’1 and keeps a reversible capacity of 174 mAh g and keeps a reversible capacity of 174 mAh gâˆ’1 after 50 cycles. A large capacity loss and the unique charge curve during the first cycle both indicate that Li[Li after 50 cycles. A large capacity loss and the unique charge curve during the first cycle both indicate that Li[Li0.23 Ni Ni0.15 Mn Mn0.62 ]O ]O2 was changed into Ni was changed into Ni0.2 Mn Mn0.8 O O2 due to irreversible oxygen loss and â€œrich lithiumâ€� extraction. The cathodeâ€™s high capacity results from the reversible conversions between Ni due to irreversible oxygen loss and â€œrich lithiumâ€� extraction. The cathodeâ€™s high capacity results from the reversible conversions between Ni0.2 Mn Mn0.8 O O2 and LiNi and LiNi0.2 Mn Mn0.8 O O2 . Both Ni . Both Ni0.2 Mn Mn0.8 O O2 and LiNi and LiNi0.2 Mn Mn0.8 O O2 keep a stable framework during cycling due to two suggested reasons: (1) some transition metal atoms migrate into the lithium layers and play as the supports between the transition metal layers; (2) 1/4 Mn keeps a stable tetravalent state during cycling and then weakens the Jahn-Teller effect of Mn keep a stable framework during cycling due to two suggested reasons: (1) some transition metal atoms migrate into the lithium layers and play as the supports between the transition metal layers; (2) 1/4 Mn keeps a stable tetravalent state during cycling and then weakens the Jahn-Teller effect of Mn3+ . The predicted reversible capacity of Li[Li (1âˆ’2x )/3x (2âˆ’x )/3 ]O2 (0 < (0 < x < 0.5) increases from 229 to 280 mAh g < 0.5) increases from 229 to 280 mAh gâˆ’1 with increasing with increasing x value, and fairly coincides with the experimental results in this work and literature.Keywords Lithium-rich layered oxide cathode lithium-ion battery References 1. simple-head.ce:title: Structure and Delithiation/Lithiation of the Lithium-Rich Layered Oxide Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 as Cathode Material

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The typical lithium-rich layered oxide Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 is synthesized via a solid state reaction, and is used as the cathode material for lithium-ion batteries. In situ X-ray diffraction analyses reveal the formation process of Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 crystallites with C2/m monoclinic symmetry. The Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 cathode delivers an initial discharge capacity of 221 mAh g−1 and keeps a reversible capacity of 174 mAh g−1 after 50 cycles. A large capacity loss and the unique charge curve during the first cycle both indicate that Li[Li0.23Ni0.15Mn0.62]O2 was changed into Ni0.2Mn0.8O2 due to irreversible oxygen loss and “rich lithium” extraction. The cathode’s high capacity results from the reversible conversions between Ni0.2Mn0.8O2 and LiNi0.2Mn0.8O2. Both Ni0.2Mn0.8O2 and LiNi0.2Mn0.8O2 keep a stable framework during cycling due to two suggested reasons: (1) some transition metal atoms migrate into the lithium layers and play as the supports between the transition metal layers; (2) 1/4 Mn keeps a stable tetravalent state during cycling and then weakens the Jahn-Teller effect of Mn3+. The predicted reversible capacity of Li[Li(1−2x)/3Ni x Mn(2−x)/3]O2 (0 < x < 0.5) increases from 229 to 280 mAh g−1 with increasing x value, and fairly coincides with the experimental results in this work and literature.
